---
title: "Best Practices for Agentic Coding: Working Effectively with AI Coding Agents"
author:
  - name: Chris Endemann
    email: endemann@wisc.edu
date: 2026-03-09
date-format: long
image: "../../../images/agentic-coding.png"
categories:
  - Guides
  - GenAI
  - LLM
  - Agentic coding
  - Software development
  - Best practices
  - Security
---

AI coding agents — tools that can autonomously write, edit, run, and test code on your behalf — are rapidly changing how software gets built. The space is crowded: [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) (Anthropic's CLI-based agent), [GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) (which now supports agentic workflows and Claude as a model), [Cursor](https://www.cursor.com/), [Windsurf](https://windsurf.com/), [Augment Code](https://www.augmentcode.com/), and [GitLab Duo](https://about.gitlab.com/gitlab-duo/) are among the most prominent options, with new entrants appearing regularly. The core principles — safety, context management, cost control — apply across all of them.

This guide focuses on **Claude Code** and **GitHub Copilot** (with Claude as the model) as representative examples, since they illustrate the key trade-offs well. We'll cover security considerations, practical strategies for getting the most out of agentic tools, and how to avoid burning through your budget or accidentally nuking your repo.

## What is agentic coding?

Traditional AI code assistants (like early GitHub Copilot or ChatGPT) work in a simple loop: you ask, they suggest, you accept or reject. **Agentic** coding tools go further. They can:

- Read and navigate your entire codebase
- Execute shell commands and run tests
- Edit multiple files in a single pass
- Iterate on their own output (fix errors, re-run tests, refine)
- Operate semi-autonomously over multi-step tasks

This is powerful, but it also means these tools have real access to your system — and the potential to do real damage if not managed carefully.

## The many ways to use Claude for coding

Before diving into best practices, it's worth clarifying the landscape. "Claude" shows up in several different coding contexts, and they have very different capabilities, risk profiles, and costs. This is a common source of confusion.

### Claude.ai (Web UI)

The [claude.ai](https://claude.ai) chat interface is where many people start — and it's more capable than you might think. While it has **no direct access to your local computer**, you can connect GitHub repositories directly, giving Claude full read access to your codebase without any copy-pasting.

**Strengths:**

- Zero local setup, zero risk to your local system
- **Connect GitHub repos** directly — Claude can browse your full codebase, understand project structure, and answer questions with full context
- Great for brainstorming architecture, reviewing code, debugging logic, or generating starter code
- Artifacts feature lets you preview and iterate on generated code, HTML, or diagrams
- Projects feature lets you upload files and set custom instructions for persistent context across conversations

**Limitations:**

- Claude can read your connected repo but **can't push changes, run tests, or execute commands** — you still apply changes manually
- Connected repos are GitHub-only (no GitLab or Bitbucket support currently)
- No agentic execution loop (can't run code, see errors, and iterate autonomously)
- Context window limits still apply — very large repos may not fit entirely in context

**Best for:** Code review, architecture discussions, understanding unfamiliar codebases, generating code you'll integrate manually, and onboarding to new projects. The connected repo feature makes this dramatically more useful than plain copy-paste — Claude can see your whole project, not just the snippet you remembered to include.

### Claude Code (CLI agent)

[Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/) is Anthropic's **terminal-based agentic tool**. It runs in your shell and can read your codebase, execute commands, edit files, and iterate on its own output — but it asks permission before doing most things.

**Strengths:**

- Full agentic capability: reads files, writes code, runs tests, fixes errors, commits — all in a loop
- Works across your entire project (not just open files)
- **Strong default guardrails** — prompts you to approve/deny each tool action (file edits, shell commands, etc.) before it executes. You stay in the loop by default.
- `CLAUDE.md` project file for persistent instructions and project-specific guardrails
- Configurable autonomy: you can loosen permissions (auto-approve reads, or even all actions) as you build trust, or tighten them for sensitive work
- Supports extended thinking for complex reasoning tasks

**Limitations:**

- CLI-first — steeper learning curve if you're not comfortable in the terminal
- Direct shell access means the security surface *potential* is high, even though defaults are conservative (see [Security section](#security-protecting-your-environment) below)
- The permission prompts can feel slow if you're used to more autonomous tools — there's a trade-off between safety and flow
- Pay-per-token via Anthropic API, which can add up during long sessions

**Best for:** Multi-step feature development, large refactors, debugging complex issues, any task that benefits from the agent being able to run and test its own code.

### Claude Code on the web (browser-based IDE)

Claude Code also runs in **browser-based development environments** — you get the same agentic capabilities without installing anything locally. This includes Anthropic's hosted environment and integrations with cloud IDEs.

**Strengths:**

- Full Claude Code agent capabilities in a browser tab
- No local setup required — useful for quick starts or when you're not on your main machine
- The remote environment is sandboxed by default, reducing risk to your local system
- Good for trying out Claude Code without committing to a CLI workflow

**Limitations:**

- Dependent on network connectivity and cloud environment availability
- May have resource limits (compute, storage, session duration) compared to running locally
- Less control over the environment compared to your own machine or container

**Best for:** Getting started with agentic coding, working from machines where you can't install CLI tools, team demos, and sandboxed experimentation.

### GitHub Copilot (with Claude as the selected model)

GitHub Copilot is an **IDE-integrated** assistant. When you select Claude as the backing model, you get Claude's reasoning quality but through **GitHub's interface and permission model**.

**Strengths:**

- Familiar IDE experience (VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) — no context switching
- Inline code completions as you type
- Chat panel for longer questions and explanations
- Agent mode (newer feature) can make multi-file edits and run terminal commands within the IDE
- Copilot coding agent can work asynchronously — you assign a GitHub issue and it opens a PR
- Subscription-based pricing is more predictable than pay-per-token

**Limitations:**

- Agent mode is still maturing — may struggle with very large or complex multi-step tasks compared to Claude Code
- Less granular permission controls — Copilot agent mode tends to just go, which is convenient but gives you less oversight than Claude Code's approve/deny model
- When using Claude as the model, you're getting Claude's intelligence but GitHub's tooling — the permission model, context handling, and capabilities are GitHub's, not Anthropic's
- Premium model requests (including Claude) may have usage limits depending on your subscription tier

**Best for:** Inline completions, quick edits, code review, and developers who prefer staying in their IDE. The async coding agent is great for well-defined issues (bug fixes, test coverage) that you want to hand off.

### Comparing the options

| | Claude.ai (Web) | Claude Code (CLI) | Claude Code (Browser) | Copilot + Claude (IDE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Agentic capability** | None | Full (conservative defaults) | Full (sandboxed) | Moderate (fewer guardrails) |
| **System access** | Read-only (connected repos) | Direct shell | Sandboxed remote | IDE-mediated |
| **Multi-file edits** | Manual (you apply changes) | Native | Native | Agent mode only |
| **Security risk** | Minimal | Highest (local) | Low (sandboxed) | Moderate |
| **Setup** | None | CLI install + API key | Browser login | IDE extension |
| **Cost model** | Free tier / Pro subscription | Pay-per-token (API) | Pay-per-token (API) | Subscription + usage |
| **Best for** | Learning, brainstorming, review | Feature dev, refactors | Quick start, sandboxed work | Daily coding, completions |

In practice, many developers use **multiple surfaces**: Claude.ai for brainstorming and review, Claude Code (CLI or browser) for multi-step feature work, and Copilot for inline completions throughout the day.

### What the same task looks like across surfaces

To make this concrete, let's walk through the same scenario — *"I have a repo on GitHub and I want Claude to add a utility function, write tests, and open a PR"* — across each surface.

#### Claude.ai (Web UI)

1. Go to [claude.ai](https://claude.ai), start a new conversation
2. Connect your GitHub repo (Settings → Connected accounts → GitHub → select repo)
3. Ask: *"Look at `src/utils/` and add a `slugify` function that matches the style of existing utilities. Also write tests."*
4. Claude browses your repo, generates the code and tests in the chat
5. **You** copy the code into your editor, create a branch, commit, and open the PR yourself

**Friction:** You're the middleware for applying changes — every edit goes through your clipboard. But notice what's *not* here: permission prompts. Claude.ai just answers. There's no approve/deny flow because there's nothing risky happening — Claude can't touch your system. This makes the web UI feel faster and more fluid for the conversational part, even though you do more manual work on the integration side.

#### Claude Code (CLI)

```bash
# Clone and navigate to your repo
git clone git@github.com:yourorg/yourrepo.git && cd yourrepo

# Start Claude Code
claude

# Now you're in an interactive session. Type your request:
> Look at src/utils/ and add a slugify function that matches the style
> of existing utilities. Write tests too. Create a branch, commit, and
> open a PR when you're done.
```

Claude Code will:

1. Read your existing utils to understand the style
2. **Ask permission** to create a new file → you approve (or it auto-approves if you've configured that)
3. Write the function
4. **Ask permission** to create the test file → you approve
5. Write tests
6. **Ask permission** to run the tests → you approve
7. Run `pytest` (or whatever your test runner is), see results
8. If tests fail, iterate — fix the code, re-run
9. **Ask permission** to create a branch and commit → you approve
10. **Ask permission** to push and open a PR → you approve

**Friction:** The permission prompts keep you in the loop but slow things down. You can reduce this by configuring auto-approve for low-risk actions (file reads, test runs) in your settings or `CLAUDE.md`. The sweet spot is usually: auto-approve reads and test execution, manually approve writes and git operations.

#### GitHub Copilot (Agent mode in VS Code)

1. Open your repo in VS Code with the Copilot extension installed
2. Open the Copilot chat panel (Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+I)
3. Select agent mode, choose Claude as the model
4. Type: *"Add a slugify function to src/utils/ matching the existing style. Write tests."*

Copilot will:

1. Read relevant files
2. Create/edit files directly — **no permission prompt by default** in many configurations
3. May run tests if it decides to (or you can ask it to)
4. You review the changes in VS Code's diff view
5. **You** handle the git workflow (branch, commit, push, PR) — or use the Copilot coding agent for that

**Friction:** The IDE experience is smooth but you have less visibility into *why* the agent made certain choices. Agent mode is also still evolving — for complex multi-step tasks it may not iterate as effectively as Claude Code's loop. The upside is zero context-switching: you're already in your editor.

#### GitHub Copilot (Coding agent — async)

1. Go to your repo on GitHub.com
2. Create an issue: *"Add a slugify utility function to src/utils/ with tests"*
3. Assign the issue to Copilot (or comment `@copilot` on the issue)
4. Walk away — Copilot works in a cloud environment

Copilot will:

1. Create a branch
2. Implement the function and tests
3. Open a PR referencing the issue
4. You get a notification when the PR is ready to review

**Friction:** This is the most hands-off option, but you have the least control during execution. Works best for well-scoped, clearly described issues. If the task is ambiguous or requires judgment calls, you may end up doing multiple rounds of PR review and comments to guide it.

#### Key takeaway

The same task ranges from fully manual (Claude.ai — you apply every change) to fully hands-off (Copilot coding agent — you just review the PR). But "more powerful" doesn't always mean "smoother experience."

Counterintuitively, **Claude.ai can feel less friction than Claude Code CLI** for many tasks — the web UI just *answers*, with no permission prompts, no approve/deny flow, and no risk of it running a bad command. You lose the ability to have Claude execute things directly, but you gain a frictionless conversation. Claude Code CLI is more capable, but its default guardrails (which exist for good reason — it has real shell access) mean more interruptions.

Across all the options, the trade-off is between **control** and **flow**:

- **Claude.ai** — most fluid conversation, but you do all the manual work
- **Claude Code CLI** — most capable, but defaults to the most permission prompts. You can loosen these as you build trust.
- **Copilot agent mode** — fewer interruptions than Claude Code, but less granular control and less effective on complex multi-step tasks
- **Copilot coding agent (async)** — most hands-off, but least control during execution

Pick based on the task. Sensitive work or unfamiliar codebase? Claude Code's guardrails are a feature. Quick question about your repo? Claude.ai with a connected repo is hard to beat. Cranking through well-understood changes? Copilot's flow might be preferable.

## Cloud and remote deployment options

Beyond the developer-facing tools above, agentic coding tools can also run in server and CI/CD contexts:

- **Cloud VMs and remote servers** — run Claude Code on a remote machine over SSH. Your local system stays untouched, and you can use more powerful hardware.
- **CI/CD pipelines** — GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and similar systems can invoke Claude Code or Copilot coding agent in **ephemeral environments** that are destroyed after each run. This is the safest way to use agentic tools at scale.
- **Sandboxed containers** — Anthropic offers purpose-built sandboxed environments for running Claude Code in isolated contexts (see [Sandbox section](#3.-sandbox-when-possible) below). Third-party providers like E2B offer similar capabilities.

The key insight: **the more system access a tool has, the more carefully you need to manage it**.

## A note for GitLab users

Many teams — including many at UW-Madison — use GitLab rather than GitHub. Claude Code works with GitLab, but the integration is **less mature** than the GitHub experience. Here's what to know:

### What works well

- **Claude Code CLI with GitLab repos** — the core experience (reading code, editing files, running commands) works identically regardless of your git host. Claude Code operates on your local checkout, so the remote platform doesn't matter for day-to-day coding.
- **GitLab CI/CD integration** — Anthropic provides [official documentation for running Claude Code in GitLab CI/CD pipelines](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/gitlab-ci-cd), including merge request review and test scaffolding.
- **Git operations** — push, pull, branching, and committing all work normally since these are standard git operations.

### What's different or limited compared to GitHub

- **No native GitLab integration in Claude Code's Slack bot** — the Slack integration currently only supports GitHub repos. GitLab support is [an open feature request](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/21527).
- **No `@claude` mention in GitLab issues/MRs** — GitHub Copilot's coding agent lets you assign issues to Copilot or mention it in PRs. There's no equivalent native integration for GitLab yet, though [GitLab is working on it](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/issues/557820).
- **Community-built CI/CD tooling** — while official docs exist, you may find yourself using [community solutions](https://github.com/RealMikeChong/claude-code-for-gitlab) to replicate the smoother GitHub Actions experience.
- **Self-hosted GitLab** — if your institution runs a self-hosted GitLab instance, be aware that Claude Code sends code context to Anthropic's API for processing. This may raise compliance concerns depending on your institution's data policies. There is currently no self-hosted or on-premise option for Claude's inference.

### Practical advice for GitLab teams

- The CLI workflow is essentially identical — focus your setup effort on CI/CD integration
- For MR review automation, use Claude Code in your `.gitlab-ci.yml` with the `claude -p` (prompt) flag for non-interactive pipeline usage
- If your institution has data sensitivity requirements, check with your IT governance team before sending code to external APIs — this applies to **all** cloud-based AI coding tools, not just Claude

## Security: Protecting your environment

This is the "box of worms" section. When you give an AI agent access to your terminal, you're trusting it with everything your shell user can do. Here's how to limit the damage.

### 1. Scope your tokens and credentials

The general advice — "don't store secrets in plain text" — isn't enough when an AI agent has shell access. The agent can read environment variables, inspect dotfiles, and run commands like `cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa` or `echo $GITHUB_TOKEN`. You need to make credentials **structurally inaccessible**, not just hidden.

#### Use separate, minimal-scope credentials

- **Create a dedicated GitHub token** (fine-grained personal access token) scoped to only the repos and permissions the agent needs. If it only needs to push to one repo, don't give it access to your entire GitHub account.
- **Use a bot account** for agent-driven git operations rather than your personal credentials. If something goes wrong, you revoke the bot — not your own access.
- **Never reuse your daily-driver SSH keys** in an agentic environment. Generate a dedicated deploy key for the specific repo.

#### Isolate credentials outside the agent's reach

The most reliable way to prevent an agent from accessing credentials is to **not have them in the environment at all**:

- **Use a credential helper for git** instead of tokens in environment variables. Git credential managers (like `git-credential-store` pointed at a file outside the mounted volume, or `gh auth` configured on the host) can provide auth without exposing the token to the shell environment.
- **In containers**, don't pass secrets as environment variables (`-e GITHUB_TOKEN=...`). Instead, use Docker secrets, mount a read-only credential socket, or configure git to use a credential helper on the host that the container calls out to.
- **Use SSH agent forwarding** cautiously — it gives the container access to your SSH key for connections but doesn't expose the key file itself. This is better than copying keys into the container, but the agent can still use the forwarded key for the duration of the session.
- **For API keys** (like `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`), the agent obviously needs this one to function. But limit the scope: set spending limits on the API key, and use a separate key from your personal/production one.

#### Filesystem hygiene

- Add `.env`, `credentials.json`, `*.pem`, `*.key`, `.netrc`, and similar patterns to `.gitignore` **before the agent ever runs**
- In containers, mount your project directory but **not your home directory**. This keeps `~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`, `~/.config/gh`, and other credential stores out of reach.
- Use read-only mounts (`-v $(pwd):/workspace:ro`) for any directories the agent only needs to read

#### Rotate and audit

- **Rotate tokens regularly**, especially any used in agentic contexts
- **Audit agent sessions** — review what commands were run and what files were accessed
- **Set token expiration dates** — fine-grained GitHub tokens support this natively

### 2. Use the permission system

Claude Code has a built-in permission model. **Use it.**

- Start in the default mode where each action requires approval
- Only escalate to auto-approve for specific, low-risk actions (e.g., file reads, grep)
- Be cautious with blanket auto-approve for shell commands — a single `rm -rf` or `git push --force` can ruin your day
- Review what the agent wants to do before approving, especially for destructive operations

### 3. Sandbox when possible

Running an AI agent on your bare metal dev machine means it has access to everything you do — your SSH keys, browser cookies, credentials, other projects, system configs. Sandboxing limits the blast radius. But not all sandboxes are equal.

#### Standard containers (Docker, Podman)

A standard container gives you filesystem and process isolation. You mount your project directory into the container, install your toolchain, and run the agent inside. This is the approach most developers are already familiar with.

**Pros:**

- You control exactly what's in the container (OS, packages, toolchain)
- The agent can't access your host filesystem outside the mounted volume
- Easy to rebuild from scratch if something goes wrong
- Works with any agentic tool, not just Claude Code

**Cons:**

- You have to set it up yourself — write a Dockerfile, manage dependencies, handle GPU passthrough if needed
- Network access is usually unrestricted by default (the agent can still `curl` your internal services, call APIs, etc.)
- You're responsible for security hardening (dropping capabilities, read-only mounts, non-root users)
- Secrets management is on you — if you pass in a GitHub token via environment variable, the agent can still read it

A typical setup looks like:

```bash
docker run -it --rm \
  -v $(pwd):/workspace \
  -w /workspace \
  -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY \
  my-dev-image \
  claude
```

This gives the agent access to your project but not your home directory, SSH keys, or other projects. It's a solid baseline.

#### Purpose-built sandboxes (Anthropic's sandboxed containers, E2B, etc.)

Anthropic and third-party providers offer sandboxing solutions specifically designed for agentic AI workloads. These go beyond standard containers:

**Pros:**

- **Network restrictions** — can limit or block outbound network access so the agent can't exfiltrate data or hit unexpected endpoints
- **Filesystem snapshots** — some solutions snapshot the filesystem before the agent runs, making it trivial to roll back
- **Pre-configured for agentic use** — tools, permissions, and resource limits are tuned for AI agent workflows out of the box
- **Ephemeral by default** — the environment is destroyed after the session, so nothing persists accidentally
- **Resource limits** — CPU, memory, and time caps prevent runaway processes

**Cons:**

- Less flexibility — you may not be able to install arbitrary system packages or use specialized hardware
- Vendor-specific — ties you to a particular platform or service
- May add latency or cost compared to running locally
- Some workflows (e.g., hardware-specific builds, GPU training) may not be supported

#### Which should you use?

| Scenario | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| **Solo dev, familiar with Docker** | Standard container with your own Dockerfile — gives you full control |
| **Team environment, need consistency** | Purpose-built sandbox — reduces setup burden and enforces consistent security |
| **CI/CD pipelines** | Ephemeral containers (either standard or sandboxed) — destroyed after each run |
| **Working with sensitive data or credentials** | Purpose-built sandbox with network restrictions — limits exfiltration risk |
| **Quick local experimentation** | Even a basic container is better than bare metal; start simple |
| **Enterprise/compliance requirements** | Purpose-built sandbox — easier to audit and demonstrate security controls |

The bottom line: **any sandboxing is better than none**. If you're running an agentic tool directly on your dev machine with full shell access, you're one bad prompt away from a very bad day. A standard Docker container gets you 80% of the way there. Purpose-built sandboxes close the remaining gaps around network isolation, snapshotting, and security hardening — but they come with less flexibility and potential vendor lock-in.

For most individual developers, a Docker container with a mounted project directory and minimal credentials is the pragmatic starting point. For teams and orgs, investing in a purpose-built sandbox solution pays off in reduced risk and lower setup friction across the group.

### 4. Watch out for prompt injection and accidental destruction

- Be careful with files that contain instructions or prompts — an agent reading a malicious `README.md` or issue body could be tricked into harmful actions
- **Never** give an agent unsupervised access to production systems, databases, or deployment pipelines
- Set up **git hooks** or CI checks as safety nets (e.g., prevent force-pushes to `main`)
- Keep your work committed frequently so you can recover from mistakes

### 5. Protect against "runaway" agents

Sometimes an agent gets stuck in a loop — repeatedly trying and failing the same approach, burning tokens and making a mess. Strategies:

- Set **spending limits** on your API account
- Use `CLAUDE.md` instructions to constrain behavior (more on this below)
- Monitor token usage during sessions
- Don't hesitate to interrupt (`Ctrl+C`) and redirect

## Practical tips: Getting the most from agentic coding

### Think in features, not projects

One of the biggest lessons from working with agentic coding tools: **use them for feature-level development, not for building entire projects in one shot.**

Why? Because agents work best with clear, well-scoped requests. The less clarity you provide, the more the agent has to guess — and guessing leads to:

- Agentic loops (trying approaches, failing, trying again)
- Drift from your intended architecture
- Wasted tokens and time
- Code that technically works but doesn't match your vision

**Precise requests get precise results.** Instead of "build me a web app with auth," try:

- "Add a login form component that submits to `/api/auth/login` and stores the JWT in a httpOnly cookie"
- "Write a pytest fixture that creates a test database with the schema from `models.py`"
- "Refactor the `process_data` function in `pipeline.py` to handle the case where `input_df` has missing columns"

Each of these is a single, well-defined task that an agent can execute without ambiguity.

### Use `CLAUDE.md` to set guardrails

Claude Code reads a `CLAUDE.md` file in your project root for persistent instructions. This is one of the most underrated features. Use it to:

**Prevent runaway loops:**
```markdown
## Testing requirements
- Always run the full test suite (`pytest tests/`) after making changes
- If tests fail, fix the failing tests before moving on
- Do not push code with failing tests
```

This single instruction saves enormous headaches. Without it, the agent might push broken code, you discover test failures in CI, and then you're spending time fixing things that should have been caught locally.

**Enforce project conventions:**
```markdown
## Code style
- Use type hints for all function signatures
- Follow the existing import ordering convention
- Do not add new dependencies without asking first
```

**Limit destructive actions:**
```markdown
## Safety
- Never run `rm -rf` on any directory
- Never force-push to any branch
- Never modify files in the `config/production/` directory
- Always create a new branch for changes; never commit directly to main
```

**Provide architectural context:**
```markdown
## Project structure
- API routes go in `src/routes/`
- Business logic goes in `src/services/`
- Database models are in `src/models/`
- Tests mirror the source structure under `tests/`
```

The more context you provide in `CLAUDE.md`, the fewer agentic loops the agent needs to figure out your project.

### Manage costs

Agentic coding tools that use API tokens (like Claude Code) charge per token, and agentic workflows are token-hungry — the agent reads files, reasons through problems, writes code, runs commands, reads output, and iterates. A single focused task might use 50K–200K tokens. A sprawling, underspecified session can easily burn through 1M+ tokens.

#### What does this actually cost?

Rough estimates for Claude Code usage (as of early 2026, using Claude Sonnet as the default model):

| Usage pattern | Tokens/day (est.) | Daily cost (est.) |
|---|---|---|
| Light — a few focused tasks | 100K–300K | $0.50–$2 |
| Moderate — active feature development | 500K–1.5M | $3–$10 |
| Heavy — large refactors, multi-step debugging | 2M–5M+ | $15–$40+ |

These are rough ranges and will vary based on model choice (Opus costs more than Sonnet, Haiku costs less), codebase size (larger context = more input tokens), and how many agentic loops occur. Anthropic also offers a **Max plan** with higher rate limits for teams doing heavy agentic work.

For comparison, GitHub Copilot subscriptions run $10–$39/month depending on tier, with usage-based pricing for premium models beyond included allowances.

#### Strategies to keep costs down

- **Be specific in your prompts** — vague requests lead to more agentic loops, which means more tokens. "Add a login form" costs more than "Add a React component at `src/components/LoginForm.tsx` that posts email/password to `/api/auth/login`"
- **Use the right tier of model for the task** — use Haiku or Sonnet for straightforward tasks, reserve Opus for complex reasoning
- **Break large tasks into smaller ones** — each focused session is cheaper than one sprawling conversation that loses context and re-reads files
- **Use `CLAUDE.md`** to provide project context upfront — this reduces the amount of exploration the agent needs to do
- **Set spending alerts and hard limits** on your Anthropic API account
- **Monitor session token usage** — Claude Code shows token counts in the session, so you can see when things are getting expensive and redirect

#### Energy and environmental considerations

It's worth acknowledging that agentic coding is more compute-intensive than traditional code completion. Each agentic loop involves multiple LLM inference calls, each of which consumes GPU compute (and the energy that powers it). A heavy day of agentic coding might involve hundreds of inference calls.

The environmental cost of AI inference is an active area of research and debate. Some perspective:

- A single LLM inference call uses roughly 0.001–0.01 kWh of energy (varies widely by model size and provider infrastructure)
- A heavy day of agentic coding (thousands of calls) might use 1–10 kWh — comparable to running a desktop computer for a day
- Cloud providers are increasingly powering data centers with renewable energy, but this varies by region and provider

This doesn't mean you shouldn't use agentic tools — the productivity gains can be substantial. But it's a reason to be intentional: don't let an agent spin in wasteful loops when a well-scoped prompt would get the job done in one pass. **Efficient prompting is both cheaper and greener.**

### Commit early, commit often

When working with an agentic tool:

1. **Commit before starting** — create a clean baseline you can revert to
2. **Commit after each successful change** — don't let the agent build up a massive diff
3. **Use branches** — never let an agent work directly on `main`
4. **Review diffs before pushing** — the agent writes the code, but you own it

### Review everything

Just because an agent wrote the code doesn't mean it's correct, secure, or maintainable. Treat agent-generated code with the same scrutiny you'd apply to any pull request:

- Read the diffs
- Run the tests
- Check for security issues (hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, etc.)
- Verify it matches your architectural patterns
- Make sure it doesn't introduce unnecessary complexity

## Summary

Agentic coding tools are genuinely powerful — they can dramatically accelerate feature development, help you explore unfamiliar codebases, and automate tedious multi-step tasks. But they require a different mindset than traditional code assistants:

1. **Scope your requests tightly** — features, not projects
2. **Use `CLAUDE.md`** to encode guardrails and project context
3. **Protect your credentials** — fine-grained tokens, `.gitignore`, secret management
4. **Use the permission system** — don't auto-approve everything
5. **Sandbox when possible** — containers, VMs, ephemeral CI environments
6. **Monitor costs** — set limits, be specific, use the right tool for the task
7. **Commit frequently** — keep escape hatches available
8. **Review everything** — you're the engineer; the agent is a very fast intern

The technology is moving fast, and best practices will continue to evolve. The core principle stays the same: **give agents the minimum access they need, provide maximum clarity in your instructions, and always keep a human in the loop for decisions that matter**.

## Further reading and perspectives

Agentic coding is evolving fast. Here are some of the best resources for staying current:

### Official documentation and guides

- [Best Practices for Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices) — Anthropic's official guide, covering context management, multi-session patterns, CLAUDE.md, and batch operations
- [Claude Code Sandboxing Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing) — reference for configuring Claude Code's built-in sandboxing, including OS-level primitives (Linux bubblewrap, macOS Seatbelt) and deny rules for sensitive files
- [Making Claude Code More Secure and Autonomous](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-sandboxing) — Anthropic Engineering's deep-dive into their dual-layer sandboxing architecture (filesystem + network isolation)
- [Mitigating the Risk of Prompt Injections](https://www.anthropic.com/research/prompt-injection-defenses) — Anthropic Research on defending AI agents against prompt injection, including their use of reinforcement learning to build injection robustness into Claude
- [GitHub Copilot: Meet the New Coding Agent](https://github.blog/news-insights/product-news/github-copilot-meet-the-new-coding-agent/) — GitHub's announcement of their enterprise-ready coding agent that spins up secure environments via GitHub Actions
- [GitHub Copilot Coding Agent 101](https://github.blog/ai-and-ml/github-copilot/github-copilot-coding-agent-101-getting-started-with-agentic-workflows-on-github/) — GitHub's getting-started guide for agentic workflows, including environment setup and PR creation
- [What's New with GitHub Copilot Coding Agent](https://github.blog/ai-and-ml/github-copilot/whats-new-with-github-copilot-coding-agent/) — latest updates including self-review, security scanning, and custom agents

### Community voices and analysis

- [Agentic Engineering Patterns](https://simonwillison.net/2026/Feb/23/agentic-engineering-patterns/) — Simon Willison's guide to coding practices for getting the best results from agents like Claude Code and Codex. He frames this as "expertise amplification, not expertise replacement"
- [A Guide to Which AI to Use in the Agentic Era](https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/a-guide-to-which-ai-to-use-in-the) — Ethan Mollick's updated guide arguing that "using AI" now means agents with tools, not chatbots, and that users must think in terms of Models, Apps, and Harnesses
- [How I Use Claude Code (+ My Best Tips)](https://www.builder.io/blog/claude-code) — practical walkthrough from Builder.io on real-world Claude Code workflows
- [The Complete Guide to Agentic Coding in 2026](https://www.teamday.ai/blog/complete-guide-agentic-coding-2026) — broad overview comparing tools, workflows, and team strategies
- [Redefining the Software Engineering Profession for AI](https://cacm.acm.org/opinion/redefining-the-software-engineering-profession-for-ai/) — ACM opinion piece on how AI amplifies senior talent but risks leaving junior developers without the chance to develop architectural intuition

### Tool comparisons

- [Cursor vs Windsurf vs Claude Code in 2026](https://dev.to/pockit_tools/cursor-vs-windsurf-vs-claude-code-in-2026-the-honest-comparison-after-using-all-three-3gof) — hands-on comparison arguing Cursor has the best IDE UX, Claude Code leads on deep reasoning and terminal-first workflows, and Windsurf offers the best value
- [Coding Agents Comparison: Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, and More](https://artificialanalysis.ai/insights/coding-agents-comparison) — benchmark-driven comparison with pricing breakdowns and SWE-bench scores

### Security

- [AI Coding Tools Exploded in 2025. The First Security Exploits Show What Could Go Wrong](https://fortune.com/2025/12/15/ai-coding-tools-security-exploit-software/) — Fortune's reporting on the "IDEsaster" vulnerabilities found across Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, and other tools
- [Researcher Uncovers 30+ Flaws in AI Coding Tools](https://thehackernews.com/2025/12/researchers-uncover-30-flaws-in-ai.html) — technical breakdown of the universal attack chains affecting major AI IDEs
- [Security Flaws in Claude Code Risk Stolen Data, System Takeover](https://devops.com/security-flaws-in-anthropics-claude-code-risk-stolen-data-system-takeover/) — Check Point's findings on Claude Code-specific CVEs, including hook injection and API key theft
- [AI Agent Security Risks in 2026: A Practitioner's Guide](https://blog.cyberdesserts.com/ai-agent-security-risks/) — practical guide to defending against prompt injection, credential theft, and MCP vulnerabilities

This is an area where best practices are being written in real time. What works today may be outdated in six months. Stay plugged into the communities above, and don't assume any single tool or configuration is permanently "safe."

## Questions
If you have any lingering questions about this resource, please feel free to post to the [Nexus Q&A](https://github.com/UW-Madison-DataScience/ML-X-Nexus/discussions/categories/q-a) on GitHub. We will improve materials on this website as additional questions come in.
